name: Flintville
short_description: Small public collecting area in Harford County featuring quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in an easily accessible low-elevation setting.
description:
Flintville is a modest rockhounding location in Harford County, Maryland, situated at 355 feet elevation with gently sloping terrain. The site offers opportunities to collect common mineral specimens including quartz, feldspar, and mica that are characteristic of the Piedmont physiographic province in central Maryland.

Geologically, this area sits within the Piedmont Uplands where metamorphic and igneous rocks of Precambrian to Paleozoic age have been heavily weathered and eroded. The gentle slopes and accessible terrain make Flintville suitable for family outings and beginning rockhounds. Specimens here typically come from weathered pegmatitic and granitic materials common to the region.

The low elevation and proximity to populated areas means this location experiences moderate foot traffic. Collectors should expect to find small to medium-sized mineral specimens rather than large crystal formations. The gentle terrain accessibility makes this an ideal spot for introducing children and new collectors to Maryland rockhounding without requiring specialized hiking skills or equipment.

Please respect private property boundaries and seek permission if needed. Collect responsibly and leave the area in good condition for future visitors.
history: This part of Harford County lies within the Piedmont physiographic region, where Precambrian and Paleozoic metamorphic rocks were formed during the Ordovician orogeny and later altered by regional metamorphism. The quartz and feldspar specimens found here derive from the breakdown of granitic pegmatites and metamorphic host rocks that were uplifted and exposed at the surface through millions of years of erosion.
tips: Search the gentle slopes and weathered outcrops where feldspar and quartz crystals are most exposed to surface erosion. Use a hand lens to identify feldspar (light colored, blocky crystals) versus quartz (glassy, hexagonal form). Look for areas where soil has been naturally disturbed by seasonal water runoff, as these often expose fresh mineral specimens. Small specimens are more common here than large crystals, so examine ground carefully and bring a collecting bag for multiple smaller pieces.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 355 feet with gentle slope throughout. Terrain is relatively open with mixed vegetation typical of the Piedmont region. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically less than half a mile. Footing is generally stable but can be soft in areas with high soil content after rain.
written_directions: From Bel Air (approximately 8 miles south), take MD Route 24 north toward Harford County. Follow local roads toward the Flintville area in northern Harford County. Use GPS coordinates 39.70486, -76.248527 to navigate to the precise location. Parking is typically available in informal pullouts or designated areas near collecting sites. Always confirm local access and obtain permission if the area is private property before collecting.
road_conditions: Paved local roads with gravel/dirt access to collecting areas. Standard passenger vehicles can typically access parking areas. No high-clearance vehicle required.
parking_notes: Informal parking in pullouts or designated areas near the collecting site. Limited formal parking infrastructure; plan to park on roadsides or small cleared areas.
collection_rules: Respect all private property boundaries and obtain permission before collecting on private land. Collect for personal use only. Do not remove large amounts of material or use motorized equipment. Leave the area in good condition for future visitors.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Obtain permission on private land
No motorized equipment
Respect all property boundaries
Leave area in good condition
nearest_services: Bel Air, approximately 8 miles south, has gas stations, restaurants, and retail shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved approach to collecting areas but short walking distances required. Sturdy casual footwear recommended. No wheelchair access to collecting areas due to uneven ground and natural terrain.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can you find rockhounding near Flintville, Maryland?
faq.answer: At Flintville you can find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. These minerals are common in the Piedmont region where metamorphic and granitic rocks have weathered over geologic time. Most specimens are small to medium-sized pieces rather than large crystal formations.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Flintville free or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Flintville is free and no permit is required, though you must obtain permission if collecting on private property. Always confirm access before visiting and respect all property boundaries in the area.

faq.question: Is Flintville rockhounding site suitable for beginners and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Flintville is excellent for beginners and families. The easy terrain with gentle slopes, short walking distances, and accessible collecting areas make it ideal for children ages 6 and up. No specialized hiking or technical skills are required.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Flintville, Maryland?
faq.answer: The best rockhounding seasons at Flintville are March through May and September through November. These months offer mild temperatures and lower humidity, making outdoor collecting comfortable. Summer heat can be intense and winter weather unpredictable in this region.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Flintville?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens to identify feldspar and quartz, a small hammer or chisel for specimen extraction, sturdy work gloves to handle sharp material, and a collecting bag. Wear comfortable walking shoes and bring water. A camera is useful for documenting specimens found.

faq.question: How far is Flintville rockhounding site from Baltimore and other nearby towns?
faq.answer: Flintville is located approximately 8 miles north of Bel Air in Harford County and about 25 miles north of Baltimore. Bel Air has gas stations and restaurants for visitor services. The site is easily accessible via MD Route 24 from populated areas in central Maryland.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Flintville, Maryland. Free public collecting on easy terrain perfect for families and beginners in Harford County.
